turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Today's orders of the Court have been duly entered and certified and filed with the clerk. Justice Breyer has the opinion this morning in case 15-5991, Shaw versus United States.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Lawrence Shaw, the petitioner in this case, obtained identifying numbers of a bank account at the Bank of America, the account of a customer and he wrongly used those numbers to transfer funds in the account to other institutions from which he could obtain, and he did obtain the customer's funds. He was convicted of violating a federal statute that makes it a crime, knowingly, to execute a scheme to defraud a financial institution like a bank. The Ninth Circuit affirmed his conviction. Now, here in this Court, he is making a set of arguments, most of which amount to variations on one basic theme. He says he intended to cheat just the depositor in the bank He did not intend to cheat the bank. Hence, the statutory provision does not apply to him. They are interesting arguments, but we do not find them convincing. First, Shaw says his scheme was not one to defraud a bank because it was a scheme to take bank customer property, not bank property. The bank, however, and not just its customer, has property interest in customers' accounts. A scheme fraudulently to obtain funds from a bank depositor's account is also a scheme fraudulently to obtain property from a financial institution, the bank. At least where, as here, the defendant knew that the bank held the deposits, the funds obtained came from the deposit account, and the defendant misled the bank in order to obtain that money. Second, Shaw says he did not intend to cause the bank any financial loss. Indeed, the bank may be reimbursed, perhaps, through forms of insurance for any losses that it suffered. The statute, however, read in light of a much relevant historical authority, we think, does not insist at all that the bank suffer ultimate financial harm. Third, Shaw argues that whatever the true state of property law, he did not know that the bank had a property interest in the customer's accounts. But long-standing legal authority again convinces us that the government need not prove that a defendant understands all those niceties of bank-related property law. And a contrary view of the statute will risk random or arbitrary results. One person convicted and the other not just because one knew -- one of them knew these very technical things about banking law. That does not seem what the statute intended. Fourth, Shaw points out that cheating the bank was not his purpose. But the statute makes the state of mind that is forbidden, a state of mind of knowing execution of a scheme to defraud. It does not insist that cheating the bank and not just cheating the bank's customers be the defendant's purpose. Finally, and separately, Shaw argues that the judge's jury instructions were erroneous. We leave it to the Ninth Circuit to determine whether he properly presented his jury instruction argument to that court. If so, whether the instructions were lawful, and if not, whether any error was harmless. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, the judgment of the Ninth Circuit is vacated and the case is remanded.
